name: Quotient Field of an Integral Domain
name_norm: quotient field of an integral domain
statement: The quotient field K(R) of an integral domain R is the localization R[U^{-1}] where U = R \ {0}.

name: Zero in localizations
statement: a. If m ∈ M, then m = 0 if and only if m goes to zero in each localization M_m at a maximal ideal m of R. b. M = 0 if and only if M_m = 0 for each maximal ideal m of R.
proof_sketch: a. The annihilator I of m is not contained in m iff m becomes zero in M_m. m=0 iff I = R iff I is contained in no maximal ideal (using Zorn's lemma). b. Apply a to every element of M.

name: Definition of Normal Domain
statement: An integral domain R is normal if it is integrally closed in its fraction field.

name: General Nullstellensatz
statement: Let R be a Jacobson ring. If S is a finitely generated R-algebra, then S is a Jacobson ring. Moreover, if n ⊂ S is a maximal ideal, then m = n ∩ R is a maximal ideal of R, and S/n is a finite extension field of R/m.
proof_sketch: The proof proceeds by reducing to the case S = R[x] using Lemma 4.20 and induction on the number of generators. Given a prime P of R[x] and a nonzero b in (R[x])/P with ((R[x])/P)[b⁻¹] a field, one shows (R[x])/P is a field. For the second statement, S/n is a field; its subring R/m is a domain integral over a field, hence a field, and the finite generation makes S/n a finite extension.

name: Rabinowitch's Trick (Jacobson Criterion)
statement: For a ring R, the following are equivalent: (a) R is a Jacobson ring. (b) If P is a prime ideal of R and S = R/P contains a nonzero element b such that S[b⁻¹] is a field, then S is a field.
proof_sketch: (a) ⇒ (b): R Jacobson implies S is Jacobson, so the intersection of maximal ideals of S is zero. In S[b⁻¹], primes correspond to primes of S not containing b. Since S[b⁻¹] is a field, b lies in every nonzero prime ideal of S, so (0) is maximal and S is a field. (b) ⇒ (a): Let Q be prime, I the intersection of maximal ideals containing Q. If I ≠ Q, pick x ∈ I \ Q and apply (b) to S = R/Q with b = x + Q. One shows S[x⁻¹] is a field, forcing S to be a field and contradicting that Q is not maximal.

name: Family as Morphism
statement: A family of varieties or algebras is a morphism; if φ: X → B is a morphism of varieties, the preimages of points of B (called fibers) are varieties that vary in a family parametrized by the points of B.

name: Radical of Annihilator and Properties of Parameter Ideals
name_norm: radical of annihilator and properties of parameter ideals
statement: Let M be a finitely generated module over a ring R, and q an ideal. Then rad(ann(M/qM)) = rad(q + ann M). In particular, if (R, m) is local: (a) q is a parameter ideal for M iff q + ann M ⊇ m^n for some n ≫ 0 iff q is a parameter ideal for R/(ann M); (b) for a short exact sequence 0 → M' → M → M'' → 0, q is a parameter ideal for M iff it is a parameter ideal for both M' and M''; (c) dim M is the minimal number of generators of a parameter ideal for M.
proof_sketch: To prove the radical equality, by Corollary 2.12 it is enough that a prime P contains ann(M/qM) iff P ⊇ q + ann M. By Proposition 2.1, P ⊇ ann(M/qM) iff (M/qM)_P ≠ 0. Nakayama's lemma gives (M/qM)_P = M_P/q_P M_P ≠ 0 iff M_P ≠ 0 and q_P ⊆ P_P. By Proposition 2.1 this holds iff P ⊇ ann M and P ⊇ q, i.e. P ⊇ q + ann M. Part (a) follows because rad(q+ann M)=m iff it contains a power of m, and ann(R/(ann M)/q(R/(ann M))) = q+ann M. Part (b) uses the induced exact sequence M'/qM' → M/qM → M''/qM'' → 0; finite length lifts from M' and M'' to M. Part (c): dim M = dim R/(ann M), so Corollary 10.7 via (a) yields the minimal number of generators.

name: Discrete valuation ring (DVR)
statement: A discrete valuation ring (DVR) is a one-dimensional regular local ring; equivalently, the valuation ring of a discrete valuation.

name: Dimension equals transcendence degree for affine domains
name_norm: dimension equals transcendence degree for affine domains
statement: If R is an affine domain over a field k, then dim R = tr.deg_k R.
proof_sketch: Let d = dim R. By Noether normalization (Theorem 13.3), there exists a polynomial subring S = k[X₁, …, X_d] over which R is finite. Then tr.deg_k S = d, and since R is finite over S, tr.deg_k R = tr.deg_k S = d.

name: Artinian Property of Monomial Orders
statement: Let F be a free S-module with basis. Any monomial order on F is Artinian (every subset has a least element).
proof_sketch: If X is a set of monomials of F, the submodule generated by X is generated by a finite subset Y (since S Noetherian). The least element of Y is the least element of X because every element of X is a multiple of an element of Y.

name: Extension of Monomial Order to Terms
name_norm: extension of monomial order to terms
statement: If um and vn are terms with non-zero coefficients u, v in k, and m, n are monomials with m > n (resp. m ≥ n) then we say um > vn (resp. um ≥ vn). This is not a partial order but a convenient notation.

name: Initial Submodule
statement: For a monomial order > and a submodule M of F, in>(M) is the monomial submodule generated by {in>(f) : f ∈ M}.

name: R-linear Derivation
statement: If S is an R-algebra, a derivation d: S → M is R-linear if it is a map of R-modules. The set of all R-linear derivations from S to M is denoted Der_R(S, M).

name: Vanishing of Derivation on 1
statement: For any derivation d: S → M, d(1) = 0.

name: Dimension Formula for Polynomial Rings
statement: a) If k is a field, dim k[x₁,…,xᵣ] = r. b) For any ring R, dim R[x] = 1 + dim R. c) If P is a prime ideal of R, there exist prime ideals Q of R[x] contracting to P, and for a maximal such Q, dim R[x]Q = 1 + dim R_P.
proof_sketch: Part a follows from b by induction. For b, any chain P₁ ⊂ … ⊂ P_d in R yields a chain P₁R[x] ⊂ … ⊂ P_dR[x] ⊂ P_dR[x]+(x), giving dim R[x] ≥ 1+dim R. The reverse inequality follows from part c. For part c, first treat R a field (P=0): R[x] is a PID, maximal Q has codimension 1. In general, localize at P to assume R local with maximal ideal P; any maximal Q containing P contracts to P. Apply Theorem 10.10 to obtain codim Q = 1 + dim R.

name: Depth Lemma (Lemma 18.3)
statement: Let (R, P) be a local ring, M a finitely generated R-module, I an ideal of R, and y ∈ P. Then depth((I, y), M) ≤ depth(I, M) + 1.
proof_sketch: Let x1,...,xn generate I, set r = depth((I,y),M). By Theorem 17.4, Hi(M ⊗ K(x1,...,xn,y)) = 0 for i < r. By Corollary 17.11 and Nakayama, Hi(M ⊗ K(x1,...,xn)) = 0 for i < r-1. Theorem 17.4 then gives depth(I, M) ≥ r-1, so r ≤ depth(I,M)+1.

name: Depth of an ideal on a module
name_norm: depth of an ideal on a module
statement: Let I be an ideal of R and M a finitely generated R-module. If IM ≠ M, define the depth of I on M, written depth(I, M), to be the length of any maximal M-sequence in I. If IM = M, set depth(I, M) = ∞. When M = R, write depth(I) for depth(I, R). If R is local with maximal ideal P, the depth of M is depth(P, M).
proof_sketch: By Theorem 17.4, all maximal M-sequences in I have the same length whenever IM ≠ M, so depth(I, M) is well-defined.

name: Locality and Completion of Cohen–Macaulay Rings (Proposition 18.8)
name_norm: locality and completion of cohen–macaulay rings (proposition 18.8
statement: R is Cohen–Macaulay iff R_P is Cohen–Macaulay for every maximal ideal P of R, and then R_Q is Cohen–Macaulay for every prime Q of R. A local ring is Cohen–Macaulay iff its completion is Cohen–Macaulay.
proof_sketch: If R is CM and Q is prime, codim Q_Q = codim Q = depth(Q,R) ≤ depth Q_Q ≤ codim Q_Q by Proposition 18.2, so equality holds and R_Q is CM. If R_P is CM for all maximal P, Lemma 18.1 gives depth(P,R)=depth(P_P,R_P) and codim P = codim P_P, so R is CM. For local (R,P) with completion (R̂,P̂), codim P = codim P̂. If x₁,…,xₙ generate P, the Koszul complex over R̂ is K̂ = R̂ ⊗ K(x₁,…,xₙ). Flatness (Theorem 7.2b) and Nakayama (Theorem 7.2a) ensure nonzero finitely generated R-modules remain nonzero after tensoring, so depth(P,R)=depth(P̂,R̂) by Theorem 17.4.

name: Koszul complex (for a map to R)
name_norm: koszul complex (for a map to r
statement: The Koszul complex is associated to a sequence of elements of R, or equivalently to a map from a finitely generated free R-module F to R.
proof_sketch: Standard construction using exterior powers; not detailed here.

name: Boundaries, Cycles, and Homology
statement: In a complex F, the elements of the image of φ_{i+1} are called boundaries, the elements of the kernel of φ_i are called cycles. The homology of F at F_i, denoted H_i(F), is the quotient module (ker φ_i) / (im φ_{i+1}).

name: Module as a Complex
statement: An R-module M may be considered as a complex with only one nonzero term (all other terms zero).

name: Limit of a Spectral Sequence
statement: Given a spectral sequence, define submodules 0 = 1B ⊂ 2B ⊂ … ⊂ rB ⊂ … ⊂ rZ ⊂ … ⊂ 2Z ⊂ 1Z = 1E such that iE = iZ / iB. Let 1Z = 1E, 1B = 0. Having defined iB and iZ for i ≤ r, define r+1Z as the kernel of the composite map rZ → rZ/rB = rE → rE, and r+1B as the image of this map. Then set 00Z = ∩r rZ, 00B = ∪r rB. The limit is 00E = 00Z / 00B.
proof_sketch: None (construction/definition).

name: Uniqueness of colimits up to isomorphism
name_norm: uniqueness of colimits up to isomorphism
statement: If a colimit of a diagram F exists, it is unique up to a unique isomorphism compatible with the cocone maps.
proof_sketch: If (A, ψ) and (A', ψ') are colimits, the universal property yields unique maps γ: A → A' and γ': A' → A with γ∘ψ_B = ψ'_B and γ'∘ψ'_B = ψ_B. Then γ'∘γ: A → A satisfies (γ'∘γ)∘ψ_B = ψ_B, as does the identity id_A. Uniqueness in the universal property forces γ'∘γ = id_A. Similarly γ∘γ' = id_A', so γ is an isomorphism.

name: Coproduct as a colimit
statement: The coproduct of a collection of objects {B_i} of A, denoted ∐_i B_i, is the colimit of the diagram consisting of the objects B_i together with only their identity morphisms.
proof_sketch: None (definition).

name: Coequalizer as a colimit
statement: The coequalizer of a pair of parallel morphisms ψ, ψ': C_1 → C_2 in A is the colimit of the diagram consisting of the objects C_1, C_2, their identity morphisms, and the two morphisms ψ, ψ'.
proof_sketch: None (definition).

name: Colimit as a functor
statement: If A has all colimits of diagrams of shape B, then colim defines a functor lim: Fun(B, A) → A from the category of functors (with natural transformations) sending a diagram F to its colimit and a natural transformation α: F → G to the unique morphism lim α: lim F → lim G induced by the composite cocone F(B) → G(B) → lim G.
proof_sketch: For a natural transformation α: F → G, the maps F(B) → G(B) → lim G form a cocone from F to lim G. The universal property of lim F gives a unique map lim α: lim F → lim G. Functoriality follows from uniqueness of the induced map.

name: Syzygy Modules
statement: Given an exact sequence 0 → N → F → M → 0 with F projective, N is called a first syzygy module of M. The nth syzygy module is defined inductively as the first syzygy module of the (n−1)st syzygy module. Syzygy modules are unique up to projective summands.
proof_sketch: N/A

name: Bounded-below complexes
statement: K+(M) is the full subcategory of K(M) consisting of bounded-below complexes F such that F_i = 0 for i sufficiently small.
proof_sketch: Definition by restriction.

name: Homology is well-defined on K(M)
statement: The nth homology module H_n(X) is well-defined for any object X in K(M), because homotopic maps induce identical maps on homology.
proof_sketch: If f ~ g, then f - g = hd + dh, so H(f) = H(g). Thus homology depends only on the homotopy class.

name: Minimal Injective Resolution
statement: An injective resolution ℰ of M is minimal if each E^n is the injective hull of ker(E^n → E^{n+1}).
proof_sketch: None (definition).

name: Five-Term Exact Sequence (Tor)
statement: If 0 → A → B → C → 0 is a short exact sequence of modules and M is a module, then there is an exact sequence Tor_1(A,M) → Tor_1(B,M) → Tor_1(C,M) → A⊗M → B⊗M → C⊗M → 0.
proof_sketch: Take a projective resolution of M and tensor with the short exact sequence; the resulting exact sequence of complexes gives a long exact sequence in homology, whose low-degree terms yield the five-term exact sequence.

name: Graded structure on the tensor algebra of a graded module
name_norm: graded structure on the tensor algebra of a graded module
statement: If R is a graded ring concentrated in degree 0 and M = ⊕ M_i is a Z-graded R-module, then T_R(M) is Z-graded with i-th graded component ⊕_{k} ⊕_{i_1+⋯+i_k = i} M_{i_1} ⊗_R ⋯ ⊗_R M_{i_k}.

name: Algebraic Independence and Transcendence Basis
statement: A subset B of a field extension K/k is algebraically independent over k if the subfield k(B) is isomorphic to the rational function field k({X_b}_{b∈B}) via the map sending X_b to b. Equivalently, for any integer n, any nonzero polynomial f(t_1,…,t_n) with coefficients in k, and any distinct b_1,…,b_n ∈ B, f(b_1,…,b_n) ≠ 0. B is a transcendence basis for K over k if B is algebraically independent and K is algebraic over k(B).

name: Matroid Basis Cardinality Lemma
statement: If B is a matroid, then all bases in B have the same cardinality.
proof_sketch: Let r be the minimal cardinality among bases. For a basis B, prove by downward induction on the number of elements B shares with a basis B' of size r. If they share r elements, B = B' since no basis contains another. If they share s < r elements, choose b' ∈ B' \ B. By the exchange property, there is a basis (B' \ {b'}) ∪ {b} of size r sharing s+1 elements with B.

name: Absolute Convergence of Local Height Series on $E(\mathbb{R})$
name_norm: absolute convergence of local height series on $e(\mathbb{r})$
statement: For an elliptic curve $E/\mathbb{R}$ given by a Weierstrass equation, there exist constants $C_1, C_2 > 0$ such that $C_1 \le \frac{|z([2^n]P)|}{|x([2^n]P)|^2} \le C_2$ for all $P \in E(\mathbb{R})$ and all $n$, and the series $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{4^n} \log|z([2^n]P)|$ is absolutely convergent.
proof_sketch: Exercise 6.9(b): bound $z([2]P)$ in terms of $x(P)$ and iterate.

name: Definition of Normal Scheme
statement: A scheme is normal if all its local rings are integrally closed.

name: Definition of Finite Type Morphism
statement: A morphism is of finite type if the extensions of local rings are finitely generated algebras.

name: Definition of Uniformizer at a Point
name_norm: definition of uniformizer at a point
statement: Fix a point p in Spec(R) with discrete valuation ord_p. A uniformizer u ∈ R for p is an element such that ord_p(u) = 1.

name: Vector Fields Agreeing on a Set
name_norm: vector fields agreeing on a set
statement: Two vector fields V and W agree on a set A if V(P) = W(P) for all P in A.
proof_sketch: None (definition).

name: Vector Field on a Sphere
statement: A vector field V on a sphere S assigns to each point P in S a vector V(P) in the tangent space T_pS, with the mapping P ↦ V(P) being continuous. For the standard sphere S^2 = {(x,y,z): x^2+y^2+z^2=1}, the tangent space is T_pS = {(a,b,c): (a,b,c)·(x,y,z) = ax+by+cz = 0}.
proof_sketch: None (definition).

name: Isomorphism of Coverings
statement: An isomorphism between coverings p: Y → X and p': Y' → X is a homeomorphism φ: Y → Y' such that p' ∘ φ = p.
proof_sketch: None (definition).

name: Reduced Zeroth Cohomology Group
statement: For a nonempty space U, the reduced 0th cohomology group H̃^0(U) is the quotient of the space of locally constant functions on U by the subspace of constant functions; thus dim H̃^0(U) = dim H^0(U) − 1, and U is connected iff H̃^0(U) = 0.
proof_sketch: None (definition).

name: Restriction of a Covering is a Covering
name_norm: restriction of a covering is a covering
statement: If p: Y → X is a covering and X' is any subspace of X, then the restriction p': Y' = p^{-1}(X') → X' is a covering.
proof_sketch: For x in X', let N be an evenly covered neighborhood in X; then N ∩ X' is evenly covered by the restriction.

name: Ramification Point
statement: A point P in X is called a ramification point for the analytic map f if its ramification index satisfies e_f(P) > 1.
proof_sketch: None (definition).

name: Poincaré Duality for Compact Oriented Manifolds
name_norm: poincaré duality for compact oriented manifolds
statement: If X is a compact oriented n-manifold, then the pairing H^k X × H^{n-k} X -> R defined by (ω, μ) ↦ ∫_X ω ∧ μ is a perfect pairing: for every linear functional φ on H^{n-k} X there exists a unique ω ∈ H^k X such that φ(μ) = ∫_X ω ∧ μ for all μ ∈ H^{n-k} X.
proof_sketch: Follows from the Duality Theorem and the nondegeneracy of the integration pairing.

name: Chain Complex of an Ordered Simplicial Complex
name_norm: chain complex of an ordered simplicial complex
statement: For an ordered simplicial complex K (partial order on vertices such that each simplex is totally ordered), CnK is the free abelian group on the n-simplices. The boundary ∂:CnK → Cn-1K is defined by ∂(v0,…,vn) = ∑_{i=0}^n (-1)^i (v0,…,v̂i,…,vn).
proof_sketch: None

name: Lemma C.10 (Surjectivity of φ*)
statement: If φ: A → A' is one-to-one, then φ*: Hom(A', ℝ) → Hom(A, ℝ) is surjective.
proof_sketch: Fix a maximal set {x_α} in A' such that no nontrivial integer linear combination lies in im(φ). For f ∈ Hom(A, ℝ) and any x ∈ A', choose n ≠ 0, y ∈ A with n x = Σ n_α x_α + φ(y). Define g(x) = (1/n) f(y). Show g is well-defined, a homomorphism, and φ*(g) = f.

name: Singular Cohomology Groups
statement: For a topological space X and abelian group G, the n-th singular cohomology group Hⁿ(X; G) is the quotient ker(δⁿ)/im(δⁿ⁻¹), where δ is the coboundary operator. Elements of ker are cocycles, elements of im are coboundaries.
proof_sketch: Verify that δⁿ⁺¹ ∘ δⁿ = 0 using ∂² = 0, so im(δⁿ⁻¹) ⊆ ker(δⁿ), thus the quotient is defined.

name: Vector Space Structure on a Field Extension
name_norm: vector space structure on a field extension
statement: For a field extension F ⊆ K, K is made into an F-vector space by defining scalar multiplication for α ∈ F and a ∈ K as α·a = αa (multiplication in K).

name: Finite and Infinite Extensions
statement: If [K : F] < ∞, then K is a finite extension of F; otherwise K is an infinite extension of F.
